# From Git to GitHub

Everything so far has been local. The daily loop (Chapter 5), the
power tools (Chapter 7), even the environment lockfile (Chapter 8) all
work on a laptop with no internet. That is by design: Git is complete
on its own. This chapter adds the one thing a local repository cannot
give you, a second copy somewhere else: a backup, a place to
collaborate, and the address you put in a paper so others can get your
code. GitHub is the most common such place, but it is not special to
Git. It is one **remote** among many.

The mechanics in this chapter were all run for real. Because the
server side of a remote is just another Git repository, the
push/fetch/pull/clone demos run against a local **bare repository**
standing in for GitHub, so the output is exact and reproducible offline.
GitHub adds hosting, a web interface, accounts, and access control on
top of identical Git behaviour (Chapters 10 and 11). Where it matters,
a box shows the read-only commands to run against the book's real repo,
`github.com/tensor-polinomics/git-github-guide`, which confirm the same
behaviour against GitHub itself. Paths are abbreviated (`~`); your
hashes will differ.

## The distributed model in one paragraph

Git is **distributed**: every clone is a full repository, with the
entire history and every branch, not a thin view of a server. There is
no privileged master copy the way older tools had. A "remote" is simply
another repository you have nicknamed so you can exchange commits with
it. The common setup, one shared remote called `origin` that everyone
pushes to and pulls from, is a *convention*, not a requirement Git
enforces. Understanding this is what makes the rest of the chapter
obvious: pushing and pulling are just moving commits between two real
repositories that happen to be on different machines.

## Connecting a repository to a remote {.tier-beginner}

A fresh local project has no remotes. You can ask, and Git lists
nothing.

```bash
$ git remote -v
(empty: a local repo has no remotes until you add one)
```

You create the repository on GitHub first (Chapter 10 walks through the
web steps), which gives you a URL. Then you tell your local repo about
it with `git remote add`, conventionally naming it `origin`. The
command prints nothing; `git remote -v` confirms it took.

```bash
$ git remote add origin git@github.com:you/minwage-did.git
$ git remote -v
origin	~/ggremotes/minwage-did.git (fetch)
origin	~/ggremotes/minwage-did.git (push)
```

The two lines (fetch and push) are the same URL here, as they almost
always are. `origin` is now a name your repo understands; nothing has
been sent yet.

::: {.callout-tip .recovery title="RECOVERY"}
Typed the wrong URL, or GitHub gave you HTTPS when you wanted SSH? You
do not remove and re-add the remote. Fix it in place with
`git remote set-url origin <correct-url>`, then re-check with
`git remote -v`. The same command is how you migrate a project to a
different host later (set the URL to the new one and push); see
host-portability below.
:::

## Push: send your commits to the remote {.tier-beginner}

The first push uploads your history and links your local `main` to the
remote's `main` so future pushes know where to go. That link is what
`-u` (short for `--set-upstream`) sets up.

```bash
$ git push -u origin main
To ~/ggremotes/minwage-did.git
 * [new branch]      main -> main
Branch 'main' set up to track remote branch 'main' from 'origin'.
```

Your five commits now exist in two places. From here on, because the
upstream is set, a plain `git push` sends whatever new commits you have
made.

```bash
$ git push
To ~/ggremotes/minwage-did.git
   1e6fe8c..e6605f6  main -> main
```

The `1e6fe8c..e6605f6` is Git reporting the range of commits it moved:
the remote's `main` advanced from the old tip to the new one.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
`git push` sends commits, and commits are forever. If you committed a
dataset or a secret earlier (Chapter 6), pushing publishes it to the
remote, and on a public repo that is effectively permanent even if you
later delete the file. Before the first push of any project, run
`git log --stat` or `git ls-files` and look at what is actually tracked.
The cheapest leak to prevent is the one still sitting on your laptop.
:::

## Clone: get a repository you do not have {.tier-beginner}

`git clone` is the mirror image of the first push: it copies a remote
repository down in full, history and all, and sets up `origin`
automatically. This is how a collaborator joins, and how *you* start on
a second machine.

```bash
$ git clone ~/ggremotes/minwage-did.git minwage-did-laptop2
Cloning into 'minwage-did-laptop2'...
done.
$ cd minwage-did-laptop2 && git log --oneline -3
c1c408c Pin the software environment with a uv lockfile
dcb18de Note headline result in README
f9a193f Add DiD analysis, report, and main table
$ git remote -v
origin	~/ggremotes/minwage-did.git (fetch)
origin	~/ggremotes/minwage-did.git (push)
```

The clone arrives with the complete log and `origin` already wired.
There is nothing thin about it: this copy could rebuild the entire
project, and the data and results regenerate from the seeded scripts
(Chapter 8) even though they were never in Git.

## Fetch and pull: bring remote work down {.tier-beginner}

When the remote has commits you do not (a coauthor pushed, or you
pushed from the other machine), you retrieve them. There are two
commands, and the difference matters. `git fetch` downloads the new
commits but does **not** touch your working files; it just updates your
view of where the remote is.

```bash
$ git fetch
From ~/ggremotes/minwage-did
   c1c408c..1e6fe8c  main       -> origin/main
$ git status
On branch main
Your branch is behind 'origin/main' by 1 commit, and can be fast-forwarded.
  (use "git pull" to update your local branch)

nothing to commit, working tree clean
```

Fetch is the safe, inspect-first option: now you can run
`git log main..origin/main` to see exactly what arrived before merging
it. `git pull` is `git fetch` followed immediately by a merge of those
new commits into your current branch.

```bash
$ git pull
From ~/ggremotes/minwage-did
 * branch            main       -> FETCH_HEAD
Updating c1c408c..1e6fe8c
Fast-forward
 README.md | 2 ++
 1 file changed, 2 insertions(+)
```

Because your `main` had no commits of its own that the remote lacked,
the update was a **fast-forward**: Git just slid your branch pointer up
to the remote's tip, no merge commit needed. When both sides have new
commits, pull has to make a real merge (or rebase), which is where
conflicts live; Chapter 12 takes that case head-on.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
The habit worth building is `git fetch` then look, not reflexive
`git pull`. Pull changes your files immediately, and if the remote and
your local branch have diverged it can drop you into a merge mid-task,
exactly when you wanted a clean tree. Fetch first, read what came in
with `git log ..origin/main`, then pull (or rebase) deliberately.
:::

## Inspecting the connection {.tier-advanced}

`git remote show origin` gives the full picture of the relationship:
the URLs, the remote's default branch, and which local branch is
configured to push and pull where.

```bash
$ git remote show origin
* remote origin
  Fetch URL: ~/ggremotes/minwage-did.git
  Push  URL: ~/ggremotes/minwage-did.git
  HEAD branch: main
  Remote branch:
    main tracked
  Local branch configured for 'git pull':
    main merges with remote main
  Local ref configured for 'git push':
    main pushes to main (up to date)
```

This is the command to reach for when a push or pull goes somewhere you
did not expect: it shows the tracking wiring in one place, so you can
see whether `main` really points at the remote you think it does.

## GitHub is one host, not the only one {.tier-advanced}

Nothing in this chapter is GitHub-specific. The same `git remote add`,
`push`, `fetch`, `pull`, and `clone` work against GitLab, Codeberg, a
university-hosted GitLab, a bare repository on a lab server reached over
SSH, or a Git server you run yourself. The URL changes; the commands do
not. Moving a project between hosts is therefore just repointing the
remote:

```bash
$ git remote set-url origin git@gitlab.com:you/minwage-did.git
$ git push
```

This portability is a real reproducibility property, not a triviality.
Your work is not locked inside one company's website. If a host
disappears, raises prices, or is blocked at your institution, every
clone is still a complete repository, and you re-home it with one
command. GitHub is the default in this book because it is where most
social-science collaborators and journals expect to find code, not
because Git needs it.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
A remote is a backup and a meeting point, not the source of truth: the
truth is the commit history, which every clone holds in full. For a
*citable* version, pushing alone is not enough, because branches move.
Tag the exact commit (Chapter 7), push the tag
(`git push origin v1.0-wp`), and cite the tag. A reader who clones and
checks out `v1.0-wp` gets the precise code behind your results, on
whatever host the repository currently lives. Chapter 15 turns that
tagged snapshot into an archived, DOI-minted release.
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Against the real repository (read-only).** These commands inspected
the book's actual GitHub remote without changing anything, and confirm
the behaviour above against GitHub rather than a local stand-in:

```bash
$ git remote -v
origin	git@github.com:tensor-polinomics/git-github-guide.git (fetch)
origin	git@github.com:tensor-polinomics/git-github-guide.git (push)

$ git ls-remote --heads origin
20738677ce7e5059b3ad5d8b7fa922bad2cb2585	refs/heads/main

$ git push --dry-run
Everything up-to-date
```

`git push --dry-run` is the safe way to preview a push: it reports the
commit range it would transfer, or says nothing needs to move, and exits
without sending anything.
:::

## What you can now do

You can connect a local project to a remote with `git remote add`, send
your history with `git push -u` and keep it current with plain
`git push`, start anywhere from a `git clone`, and bring collaborators'
work down with `git fetch` (inspect first) or `git pull` (fetch and
merge). You understand that a remote is just another full repository,
that `origin` is a convention, and that the whole workflow is portable
across hosts because Git is distributed. What you do not yet have is the
layer GitHub builds on top of this: the web interface, repositories with
READMEs and licenses, issues, and pull requests. Chapter 10 starts
there.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

You will need a free GitHub account (Chapter 10 covers signup) or any
remote you can reach. Work in a clone of the running example.

1. Create an empty repository on GitHub, then `git remote add origin`
   its URL and `git push -u origin main`. Reload the web page and
   confirm your five commits are there.
2. Clone your new GitHub repo into a second folder. Run
   `git log --oneline` in the clone and confirm the history matches.
   Explain why the clone did not need the `data/` directory.
3. In the clone, edit the README, commit, and `git push`. Back in the
   original folder, run `git fetch` then `git log main..origin/main` to
   see the incoming commit *before* merging it, then `git pull`.
4. Run `git remote show origin` and find the line that says your `main`
   pushes to the remote's `main`.
5. Tag the current commit `v0.1-practice`, push the tag with
   `git push origin v0.1-practice`, and confirm on GitHub that the tag
   appears under the repository's releases/tags view.
